sentences.text: Wilhelmina Jacobs.
London, England.
My mother was Clara Diamond.
And my father was Samuel Jacobs.
Three.
Two boys, one girl.
My eldest brother was Albert.
My youngest brother was Jack, and I-- I was in the middle.
March the 22nd, 1911.
Four.
Well, first-- well, I remember the first one was Paddington.
Well, no, it's really London.
It's not-- it's a couple of miles out of, out of West End.
My father was a cellist.
And he was a musician, a cellist--
A musician.
And he worked in--
In the musical comedies in the hippodrome in Piccadilly.
In Antwerp and in Paris.
He-- no.
He was born in Amsterdam.
Also from Holland, also born in Amsterdam.
Yes, my mother's parents were alive.
Her name was Wilhelmina Cumerman And my grandfather was Joseph Jacobs.
I beg your pardon?
Oh, my, my grandfather was in-- a diamond cutter.
And my grandmother was a housewife.
Yes.
He traveled when he was younger.
He lived in Paris first, because his-- should I go on with his elder brother?
His elder brother was born a genius.
He was a violinist.
He was a conductor of 100 men in Paris Orchestra when he was four years old.
Jack Jacobs.
Have you heard of?
He was compared to Jascha Heifetz.
They were compared to each other.
She just looked after us three kids.
Looked-- a lot of looking after.
Yes, in an apartment above an office building.
Middle, middle class.
Oh, yes.
My two, my two brothers were brought up from four years old to be musicians.
Violin and trumpet.
And I was on piano.
No.
I wanted to be a dancer.
Yes.
Later on, I went to dancing school in Kensington.
I went to school-- in private schools, kindergartens, of course, in London.
And then we moved to Southend-on-Sea, where I went to Southend Grammar School.
After grammar school, I went to Rottingdean.
That's near Brighton.
I was-- went to a convent.
Because my, my father's brother married a Christian woman, and she had a, a daughter.
And this daughter went to that school.
And she persuaded my mom and my dad to send me there, because it was a very good education.
That's the only reason.
No, not at all.
I was-- as a matter of fact, I was a favorite in the school.
Oh, yes.
No.
Yes, by eating no lobsters and no rabbit, nothing like that.
No.
No.
Yes, well they always-- they knew.
And we had special treats on those days, but not to say-- it was a tradition in the family.
It's tradition of the Jewish people that they always observe something.
Like this-- I have for luck, somebody gave me this.
I think I must have been about 14, and I can't remember who.
And I've had it ever since.
And wherever I go, this is what-- my husband doesn't even-- it's a secret.
I don't know why.
This is what I--
Well, he doesn't know.
Maybe he did once know, but--
--he forgot.
It should be like that, you know?
It broke off.
[INAUDIBLE] years.
You know what it is?
They put it on the door for, for-- Jewish people have it always on the door.
That's right.
It was a friend of mine years and years-- I must have been about 13 or 14.
And she said, I want you to keep this always with you because this is your guardian angel.
And it never left me.
I always had it.
It was whole once, but it broke off.
Not really, but I know it brings-- I know it keeps you safe and it brings you luck.
Of course, in my life, whatever I did, I've always fallen on my feet the right way.
So it must be something.
Right.
Good way to fall.
I always-- my purse.
You keep it on your body all the time.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Always-- it's always with me in my purse.
Every purse I take, I change it over.
Well, I-- I-- he may not even know.
He may know.
I don't know if he knows.
He doesn't remember things so well, and he hasn't seen it for a long time.
It's nothing really I keep secret from him.
It's just something that just happens, you know?
Yes, it's personal.
I beg your pardon?
Well, yes.
I used to do pen painting on black velvet.
It's like a pen with special thick ink.
And you pencil flowers on velvet, all sorts of decorations.
And dancing.
No, they were decorations.
You made bracelets, and necklaces, and things like that.
And cushions.
You can make whole cushions with it too.
My dancing.
I went to a dancing school in Kensington.
A professional one.
A professional dancing school.
I was about 14, 15, between the ages of that and 18.
Oh, yes.
My, my, my mother, my mother had everything to say.
My father listens.
[LAUGHTER] They were very, very good couple.
They were always called Muff and Jeff.
I never heard them have a quarrel or a bad word, in all the, in all the times we lived-- I lived with them.
It is.
[LAUGHS] He was a very, very gentle man.
He was also a gentleman.
And my mother was very friendly with everybody, and she gave good advice.
I give-- that same advice, I give to my children and to my grandchildren.
Well, my mother-- my father had nothing to do with the children.
He just worked and brought in a salary.
You know how it was in those days.
The man worked and the woman worked in the home.
And he got, he got his pocket money for cigarettes, and that was it.
That was enough for him.
My mother paid all the bills, everything.
Acrobatic and ballet.
Yes, quite a long time, but my father was a very, very old fashioned man.
I was-- I was never allowed to take a job on the stage.
He just wanted me to learn, because I wanted to learn that.
But he would never let me take a job.
He was against it, because he was-- he saw what was going on in the arts world, like it is today.
It's worse today than it was then.
Because he always used to say, if you want to get on in the film business or in the stage business, you have to go to bed the director.
[LAUGHS]
And that was the case then as well.
That's the only way to get on there.
Right.
Really, yes.
But I used to do it for friends, and you know, parties friends gave, and things like that.
I used to do it privately.
He didn't mind about that.
Oh, yes.
I had a lot-- we had lots of friends.
But I was never allowed out alone with friends.
I was taken to a party, I was taken back from a party.
Well, mostly, but a lot of my friends were much freer than I was.
I was the Prisoner of Zenda.
[LAUGHS]
My brothers used to call me that.
And what you attribute that over-protectiveness to?
Was it his-- was it your family's upbringing?
Was it something that their parents had done?
Yes.
That's the way they were brought up too.
Not really.
I didn't know what-- we kids in those days, we didn't know what that was, rebelling against your parents.
Your parents were your gods.
Everything they did, everything they said, was good.
Oh, yes.
There were Jewish children too.
I wouldn't know that now.
You know how long ago that is?
No.
The grammar school, the grammar school was the last school I attended.
It was a-- it was a very, very good school, well known for, for its-- the teaching high-class things, more than a public school.
They were advanced.
It was something that your parents paid for privately.
Yeah.
Well, I, I was around about 16.
Then I went into another school to teach the kindergarten for two years.
Yes, it was good.
I enjoyed it.
That was in Mayvale.
That's a suburb of London.
Well, my mother-- my mother said you should do that, so I did it.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, I liked that too.
And you did that for a while--
For a while, and then I went to the dancing school.
After dancing school, I wanted to learn hairdressing.
So I went in for hairdressing for a year.
And that was in Regent Street in London.
Yeah.
I had a very happy youth.
I enjoyed everything I did.
My two brothers.
Oh, yeah.
I got along better was my eldest brother than my youngest brother.
He was born on my first birthday.
He was my birthday present.
[LAUGHTER]
Well, no.
This is all a suburb.
I went with the underground home.
So you continued to live at home.
That was about 20 minutes.
After I studied to be a hairdresser, I got married.
I went to-- my, my grandparents lived in Antwerp.
And my mother said, you have to go and see your grandparents.
They're getting older.
I never did want to go to, to see them.
I didn't know them.
Yes, I saw them once or twice when I was little, because they went to live in South Africa.
But when they came back from South Africa, they lived in Antwerp again.
Diamonds.
My grandfather was in the diamond business.
Diamond cutter, diamonds-- everything in diamonds, anyway.
Johannesburg.
I still have family there.
Your parents, though, never moved there.
No.
We went there.
We went there--
--once.
That was when I was about-- you see that photo, the photo there?
I was about-- I was about three years old.
That must have been a very long journey.
Yes, it was in those days, I suppose.
I didn't notice it, of course.
Oh, yes.
It was-- you know, every kid is pleased with thinking, of liking to go to another country.
I was about 18, 19.
I took my youngest brother with me, because I wasn't allowed to travel alone.
Well, when I met my husband, we were in Antwerp to see my grandparents.
And my cousin was engaged to Louis' brother.
So she said, you know, I have to go Friday evening to my parents-in-law for dinner.
And what are you going to do?
And she said, I would like you to go with me.
I said, I'll go with you.
So I went with her to see her parents at dinner, and I saw Louis coming out of a shoemaker shop.
So she said, oh that's my fiance's brother.
But he was only about 13 at that time, so I didn't take any notice of him, of course.
And that's, that's the first time I saw him.
And how old were you then?
I was about 18.
Uh huh.
So you didn't get married then.
But I got-- I saw him once before when a few years younger, when he was 13 and I was 15.
But I didn't-- it wasn't anything special.
I just didn't take any notice.
He was so young, you know?
He was a baby.
But this time, when you went to, to see your, your--
My grandparents?
That time?
This time, I got engaged to him.
Well, he took me out at evening.
He, he had a girlfriend, so we went both together to say to her he couldn't go, because he had friends from England.
So he took-- we went out dancing.
And he was-- I didn't understand him, he didn't understand me.
But we danced.
And you know, I don't know how it happened.
But in two days time, he asked me to marry him.
And I was sleeping with an aunt at my grandparents' place, and they brought me a bowl of flowers up.
They were old flowers that they had downstairs.
I said, what's this in honor of?
She said, well, you got engaged to Louis De Groot last night.
I said, I did not.
She said, oh yes, you did.
So the-- I was so shy, I didn't even dare to say that's not true.
So I said, oh, that's possible.
So that's the way it remained.
And we got married.
[LAUGHTER] It's silly.
I won a-- I won a prize on television with this story once too, when we first came to Canada thirty years ago.
No.
I was so naive.
I was a real child.
When I was 17, I didn't even know what-- when a woman was pregnant.
When she was this big, I didn't even know it was that.
I thought it was a sickness.
I was 17.
He was a nice guy.
He's awfully nice to me.
He was a nice guy.
I hadn't ever been out with guys or anything like that, you know.
So I fell in love with him, I suppose.
If that's love like that, it's love at first sight.
[LAUGHTER]
No.
I was Jewish.
I haven't ever even thought of anything like that.
Oh, no.
My husband-- my parents were very happy about it.
But if you would have said that you were bringing home someone who was not Jewish--
Then they wouldn't have liked it.
No.
They always wanted me to marry a Jewish guy, always.
My parents-in-law.
Then I went back home.
I think for a week or two.
And then Louis came over to ask my dad my hand.
You know how that goes years ago?
You had to come and ask for your-- for the girl's hand.
My husband?
Yes.
He was scared, because he tore a pound note in half in his anxiety.
[LAUGHS]
Well, they thought he was a little bit crazy, tearing money in half.
[LAUGHTER]
But they liked him.
They liked him.
He was a nice guy.
He was polite.
Yes.
Well, that's-- they did really want me to stay by, but things don't go that way.
They saw we were going to do it, and that was it.
My father was a very sick man.
He had-- my mother would never tell us what it was.
But I guess it was cancer on the liver.
She would never tell us that but, you know, years ago, the word cancer wasn't spoken.
That was talked of as an illness.
From cancer.
Well, that wasn't too much.
We got married in a, in a, in the synagogue.
It wasn't too much--
--fuss about it.
It was a small one, I don't remember-- in Antwerp.
That was in Antwerp.
Yes.
And the next day, the next day, that same rabbi ran away with a young girl.
[LAUGHTER]
That's so much for the rabbi.
And then we went home to my mother-in-law's house.
My mother was there.
My father was very sick, so he couldn't come over.
He had to stay in England.
My mother came for a few days, and my mother-in-law made dinner.
And we had a few friends over in the house.
And that was our wedding day party.
Black-- black velvet.
Because I like black velvet, and I didn't particularly want to get married in white.
Yes.
Everybody got married in white, as far as I know.
But I liked black.
I don't think so.
I don't really think anybody in those days took any notice of things like that.
And my, my mother-in-law didn't think it was bad or anything like that.
She was, she was brought up religiously, but she didn't say anything about it.
My parents.
Well, that is, my mother was in Antwerp, and my father lived in Paris before they were married.
And then my father went to London.
And my grandparents wouldn't let my mother go over without being married.
So that's just a sort of a prayer that's made, when my father has a glove in his hand in London, and my mother has a glove in her hand in Antwerp.
So but you were-- you are legitimate.
Yes.
[LAUGHTER]
Definitely so.
[LAUGHTER]
Well, not, not too many.
I think I had an old sewing machine from my mother-in-law.
And I think I had a glass with, a water glass with six cups from one of my brothers.
And from my mother, she, she gave me a house in Antwerp, which she had there.
And from friends, a fishbowl was some fish running around in it.
And from another friend, a table.
Those sort of things, you know.
In Antwerp.
We lived in Antwerp.
You lived in the apartment that your family--
No, in the little house, we lived.
We-- oh, no.
First, we lived in-- first we lived in a little flat.
And there, there, Ronnie was born.
Nine and a half months.
Nine and a half months.
Everybody wants to make it eight and a half, but it was nine and a half.
Oh, yes.
We did want to have a big family, but when the war broke out, we decided not to bring anymore kids into the world, in case something like this could happen again.
So we stayed with two boys, and then we adopted a girl.
After the war, we adopted a girl, but was 15.
And she-- she was-- she had an aunt and uncle living around the corner.
In Ixelles, where we lived in Ixelles during the war.
Everyone?
Yes, in Belgium.
But she was-- her aunt and her uncle-- she, she was-- started to do things, and write secret letters.
And, and we couldn't manage her.
She was 15.
And she was jealous of the boys.
And I treated her better than I treated my own children, because she was adopted.
And then we just said-- then the aunt and uncle decided that we'd better send her to them.
And she wanted to go, too.
She was Jewish-- Polish Jewish child.
And she brought up, you know, different to our bringing up.
And she was better off with her aunt and her uncle.
Her parents were deported.
Yeah.
We wanted-- really, we wanted to adopt a baby girl.
But it didn't work that way.
Yeah.
Ronald is not a Jewish name.
Jack, Jack, Ronald--
No.
Ronnie is just Ronald.
And Jack is Jack Richard.
It's a silly thing to say, but my eldest son was named after a film star, which which I was crazy about.
I said, if I have a son, he's going to be named Ronald.
Ronald Colman.
If you ever heard of him.
Yes, I have.
[LAUGHS] And your second son?
That second son is, is named after my brother-- after an uncle and after my brother.
No, he was alive.
They said, you shouldn't do that, but I did.
The Sephardim have such a custom.
My brother was very proud to have him named, named after him.
And then I have a grandson.
